content.text: Every program has more stakeholders than the program manager has time to communicate with individually. Without a deliberate cadence, high-influence stakeholders receive too little information until a crisis requires their involvement, while lower-influence stakeholders receive too much. A structured communications cadence maps each stakeholder's influence and interest to a specific outreach frequency, format, and channel — ensuring the right people receive the right information at the right time without overwhelming anyone.

attrs.alt: Stakeholder engagement matrix plotting program stakeholders by influence and interest with communication strategy per quadrant

content.text: The standard tool for designing a communications cadence is the influence–interest matrix. Plotting each stakeholder on two axes determines which engagement strategy applies:
Representing stakeholders as structured data lets the program manager compute engagement strategies automatically and generate tailored communications at scale:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class EngagementStrategy(Enum):
    MANAGE_CLOSELY  = "manage_closely"   # high influence, high interest
    KEEP_SATISFIED  = "keep_satisfied"   # high influence, low interest
    KEEP_INFORMED   = "keep_informed"    # low influence, high interest
    MONITOR         = "monitor"          # low influence, low interest

@dataclass
class Stakeholder:
    name: str
    role: str
    influence: int   # 1–5 (5 = budget/launch authority)
    interest: int    # 1–5 (5 = actively involved in outcomes)
    preferred_channel: str   # "email" / "slack" / "steering" / "1:1"
    concerns: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def strategy(self) -> EngagementStrategy:
        hi_inf = self.influence >= 4
        hi_int = self.interest >= 4
        if hi_inf and hi_int:   return EngagementStrategy.MANAGE_CLOSELY
        if hi_inf and not hi_int: return EngagementStrategy.KEEP_SATISFIED
        if not hi_inf and hi_int: return EngagementStrategy.KEEP_INFORMED
        return EngagementStrategy.MONITOR

    @property
    def comms_cadence(self) -> str:
        cadences = {
            EngagementStrategy.MANAGE_CLOSELY: "Weekly 1:1 + bi-weekly steering report",
            EngagementStrategy.KEEP_SATISFIED: "Monthly executive brief + escalation-only outreach",
            EngagementStrategy.KEEP_INFORMED:  "Weekly team status + ad-hoc on milestones",
            EngagementStrategy.MONITOR:        "Quarterly update; notify on major decisions only",
        }
        return cadences[self.strategy]
Populating the register at program kickoff produces the engagement strategy and cadence for every stakeholder automatically:

# Program stakeholder register for a Claude enterprise rollout
stakeholders = [
    Stakeholder("Sarah Chen",   "CTO / Exec Sponsor",    influence=5, interest=5,
                preferred_channel="steering",
                concerns=["time-to-value", "security posture", "budget ROI"]),
    Stakeholder("James Okafor", "CISO",                  influence=5, interest=3,
                preferred_channel="email",
                concerns=["data privacy", "model access controls", "audit trail"]),
    Stakeholder("Priya Patel",  "Program Director",      influence=4, interest=5,
                preferred_channel="slack",
                concerns=["milestone adherence", "team capacity", "risk register"]),
    Stakeholder("Li Wei",       "CFO",                   influence=5, interest=2,
                preferred_channel="email",
                concerns=["budget variance", "ROI measurement"]),
    Stakeholder("Team Leads",   "Engineering / PM Leads", influence=2, interest=5,
                preferred_channel="slack",
                concerns=["sprint clarity", "dependency unblocking", "tooling"]),
]

for s in stakeholders:
    print(f"{s.name:20} | {s.strategy.value:20} | {s.comms_cadence}")

Each stakeholder has different concerns. A CISO reading a program update cares about data privacy controls and audit trails, not sprint velocity. Claude generates stakeholder-specific messages by reasoning over the stakeholder's role, concerns, and engagement strategy:

import anthropic

def generate_stakeholder_update(
    stakeholder: Stakeholder,
    program_name: str,
    program_rag: str,
    headline: str,
    top_risk: str,
    decision_needed: Optional[str] = None,
) -> str:
    """Generate a communication tailored to this stakeholder's role and concerns."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    concerns_txt = "; ".join(stakeholder.concerns) if stakeholder.concerns else "general progress"

    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Write a brief stakeholder communication for {stakeholder.name} ({stakeholder.role}).

Program: {program_name}
Overall RAG: {program_rag.upper()}
Headline news: {headline}
Top risk: {top_risk}
Decision needed: {decision_needed if decision_needed else "None"}

This stakeholder's engagement strategy: {stakeholder.strategy.value}
Their known concerns: {concerns_txt}
Preferred channel: {stakeholder.preferred_channel}

Write a {stakeholder.strategy.value.replace("_"," ")} message (≤100 words) that:
1. Leads with what matters most to this person specifically
2. Gives the RAG status with one-line rationale
3. States any decision they need to make (or omits if none)
4. Is appropriate for a {stakeholder.preferred_channel} message

Do not use generic PM language. Be direct and specific to their concerns.
"""}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text


# Example: tailored update for CISO (keep satisfied — high influence, lower interest)
ciso = next(s for s in stakeholders if s.role == "CISO")
msg = generate_stakeholder_update(
    stakeholder=ciso,
    program_name="Claude Enterprise Rollout Q3",
    program_rag="amber",
    headline="Integration build on track; Security review for Sales AI is the blocker.",
    top_risk="Sales AI RAG pipeline blocked pending security review for 3 weeks.",
    decision_needed="Approve expedited security review for Sales AI by July 14.",
)
print(msg)

With the stakeholder register in place, a full-quarter communications calendar can be generated algorithmically. The calendar shows exactly which stakeholder receives which communication type, in which week, on which channel:
Team leads; weekly status; quick asks
Exec brief; steering report; formal decisions
Sometimes — flag clearly if action needed
Yes — follow up with email summary

A cadence covers planned touchpoints, but programs generate unplanned events — a critical dependency blocked, a budget exception needed, a security finding. Unplanned communications should follow three rules:
: A blocker that threatens the launch date warrants an immediate Slack message to the program director, not an email in the weekly update.
: 'The Sales AI pilot is delayed 4 weeks because of a security review block' is more useful than 'We have a dependency issue I wanted to flag.'
: Every unplanned escalation should include at least one proposed resolution path. Arriving with only a problem transfers the entire cognitive burden to the stakeholder.
Communication Plan as a Living Document

Stakeholder influence and interest shift during a program. A director who was disengaged in Q2 becomes highly interested when a Q3 launch affects their team's operations. Review the stakeholder register at each PI planning cycle:
Has any stakeholder's influence changed (e.g. new budget authority, role change)?
Are any stakeholders showing unexpected interest — attending calls they don't usually join?
Have any stakeholders complained about receiving too much or too little information?
Have new stakeholders joined the program (new hires, acquired teams, compliance officers)?

Sending communications is not the same as being heard. A stakeholder who stops attending steering meetings, stops replying to status emails, or begins routing around the program manager to their own sources is showing that the current cadence is not meeting their needs. Track engagement signals to detect these patterns early:
Reach out for 1:1; ask if format needs change
Shorten to 3 sentences; switch to verbal briefing
Stakeholder may be disengaged or have lost trust
Investigate concerns; rebuild trust with more frequent 1:1

The most reliable signal of a failing communications cadence is when a senior stakeholder starts gathering information directly from team leads rather than from the program manager. This usually means the program manager's updates are arriving too late, at the wrong level of detail, or with gaps the stakeholder has to fill in themselves.

Once the stakeholder register and calendar are in place, the weekly dispatch can be automated. Claude generates each stakeholder's message from the current program data; the program manager reviews and approves before sending:

import anthropic
from datetime import date

def run_weekly_comms_dispatch(
    stakeholders: list[Stakeholder],
    program_name: str,
    program_rag: str,
    headline: str,
    top_risk: str,
    decisions: list[str],
    as_of: date,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Generate all weekly stakeholder messages in one pass."""
    dispatch = []
    active_today = [
        s for s in stakeholders
        if s.strategy in (
            EngagementStrategy.MANAGE_CLOSELY,
            EngagementStrategy.KEEP_INFORMED,
        )
    ]

    for s in active_today:
        decision_for_this_stakeholder = next(
            (d for d in decisions if s.role.lower() in d.lower()), None
        )
        message = generate_stakeholder_update(
            stakeholder=s,
            program_name=program_name,
            program_rag=program_rag,
            headline=headline,
            top_risk=top_risk,
            decision_needed=decision_for_this_stakeholder,
        )
        dispatch.append({
            "to": s.name,
            "role": s.role,
            "channel": s.preferred_channel,
            "strategy": s.strategy.value,
            "message": message,
            "as_of": as_of.isoformat(),
            "approved": False,   # PM reviews before sending
        })

    return dispatch


# Example dispatch run
messages = run_weekly_comms_dispatch(
    stakeholders=stakeholders,
    program_name="Claude Enterprise Rollout Q3",
    program_rag="amber",
    headline="API and RAG milestones on track; Sales AI blocked on security review.",
    top_risk="Security review not started; Sales AI pilot delayed 4 weeks.",
    decisions=["CISO: approve expedited security review by July 14"],
    as_of=date(2025, 7, 7),
)

for m in messages:
    print(f"[{m['strategy']}] → {m['to']} via {m['channel']}: {m['message'][:80]}...")
Communications During Crisis or Red Status
When the program hits Red status, the standard cadence is insufficient. A distinct crisis communications protocol takes over:
Root cause, business impact, mitigation options — one paragraph
Impact on quarter deliverables, decision needed, recommended action
Situation update, recovery plan, revised timeline
What changed, how it affects their work, what they should do differently
All stakeholders who received crisis comms
What was resolved, what changed permanently, lessons learned

The crisis communications template is prepared in advance and populated with facts when a Red event occurs. Claude drafts each layer in seconds from a structured incident description, so the program manager can focus on resolution rather than writing.

A stakeholder communications cadence translates the influence–interest matrix into a concrete weekly schedule: who receives what, when, and in what format. The stakeholder register stores this structure, the cadence algorithm builds the quarter calendar, and Claude generates tailored messages that speak to each stakeholder's specific concerns. The result is a program communication system that runs consistently without requiring the program manager to reinvent the approach every week.
